BRITS in Dubai are facing prison for “commenting on social media posts” related to Iran’s onslaught on the United Arab Emirates.

Radha Stirling, CEO of Detained in Dubai, an organisation that gives legal assistance to foreigners in the Gulf state, told The Sun that dozens of UK nationals have contacted her in the past week, seeking urgent help.





A Londoner was yesterday charged – among 20 others – under draconian cyber-crime laws, after allegedly filming Iranian missiles causing carnage – and more are expected to fall victim.

Stirling explained that the legislation is “deliberately very broad” – and in times of war it is becoming even more dangerous.

“Families of people being detained now under cyber-crime laws for taking photos and videos… are not sure what their loved ones have been charged with yet,” she said.

“We are waiting for more details, but it could be sharing news, it could be commenting on a post on social media. It is very very broad.

“And once people are charged, it is unclear what they have done wrong.”

It comes as…

The UK Foreign Office confirmed to The Sun that it is in contact with local authorities following the detention of a British man.



This comes just two weeks after the UAE Public Prosecution Office warned against publishing or circulating “rumours” amid Iranian attacks.

Stirling also pointed to three separate groups in the UAE that are now “in danger”.

First, she highlighted the cases of UK expats with personal travel bans due to unpaid debts, labour disputes, or criminal investigations.

This means that they are not allowed to leave the country despite drone and missiles strikes causing carnage across the Gulf state.

Just in the last 24 hours, six people were killed and another 131 wounded in the latest barrage.



“Many are desperate to leave but are not allowed to,” Stirling said.

Detained in Dubai has been calling on the UK to negotiate “humanitarian passes” for Brits who do not feel safe in the UAE and wish to return home.

Stirling is also concerned about Brits who were arrested in the few weeks or months before the start of the war, and have since been awaiting bail hearings in jail.

“People are awaiting trials or court dates to plead not guilty to allegations made against them,” she said.

“They don’t know when they’re going to be processed. So during a time of crisis, how is the justice system going to work in Dubai?



The UAE's cryptic cyber-crime laws THE UAE’s cyber-crime laws are among the strictest in the world. People can face fines of up to £57,000, lengthy detention, and travel bans for social media posts that authorities consider harmful to “public order”, “national unity” or the reputation of the state. Detained in Dubai warns that the legislation is “deliberately broad” – and in times of war they become even more dangerous. Almost anything critical of government policy or regional conflict can be interpreted as a crime. In practice, commentary about regional conflict, government policy or security matters can be interpreted as a criminal offence. Sharing advice about what to do and what not to do in the country, the organisation said: “Do not assume your home country’s free speech protections apply in the UAE. They do not. “Do not assume that sharing international news coverage is safe. It may not be. “Do not assume that deleting a post removes liability. It often does not.”

“With everyone’s focus elsewhere, it could be much slower than usual. It could be four or six months before they get a trial.

“The Dubai justice system has moved slowly in the past. And it is difficult for families back home in Britain to feel that they are going to get justice and that their child is going to be freed.

“It is terrifying that that there is nothing they can do to keep them safe and there is nothing that the person in detention can do either. It is so difficult when your safety is in the hands of someone else.”

Prisoners who have completed their sentences and are waiting to be freed have also fallen victim of the “preoccupied” justice system.

“They have done their time,” Stirling stressed.



“Any day beyond [their sentence] is basically arbitrary detention. Families do not want to see their loved ones in prison for another six months.

RECOMMENDED STORIES

“No one knows how long or short this war is going to be. At the moment the UAE is just saying, ‘You know, we’re busy. We don’t care.”‘

For years, Dubai has cultivated a reputation as a glamorous and attractive destination – and more than 250,000 British nationals have made it their home in recent years.

News.title: Epstein, Russia & £500k demand… 5 bombshells from Mandelson files as pressure mounts on Starmer over ‘cover-up’
News.content:
FROM his relationship with Jeffrey Epstein to demands for £500k payout, the release of the files about Peter Mandelson has piled new pressure on Keir Starmer amid accusations of a “cover up.”

Hundreds of files relating to Mandelson and his time as an Ambassador were dropped on Wednesday following the Labour grandee’s earlier arrest.





The former Labour peer was taken into custody on suspicion of misconduct in a public office after his close ties with Jeffrey Epstein were revealed in files released by the US Department of Justice.

The-72-year-old resigned from the Labour party and the House of Lords in disgrace in February after the release of the Epstein files.

He was sacked from his Washington role last September and now faces a police probe as the snowballing scandal deepens.

Mandelson has since been released from custody and his team has confirmed his passport has been returned.

Now, the Prime Minister faces mounting pressure as his decision to appoint Mandelson is scrutinised.

Opposition leader Kemi Badenoch has blasted the PM, calling the release of the documents a “cover up” after noticing sections supposed to be filled in by Sir Keir were left blank.

Mandy demanded £500k

Buried in the Mandelson files was evidence that the Labour grandee pocketed a £75,000 golden goodbye after being sacked as ambassador.

According to the documents, he first demanded £547,000 of taxpayers’ cash before settling for the final figure.



Mandelson is understood to have demanded that the Foreign Office treat him well after his sacking.

Following the release of the Epstein files he called for “maximum dignity” after he returned from the US in disgrace.

Darren Jones, Chief Secretary to the PM, has since said Mandelson should hand back his severance pay.

He insisted the payout was made to avoid even higher legal costs.



Farage ‘bridge’ with Trump

In another bombshell revelation the Mandelson files show the former US Ambassador suggested leveraging Nigel Farage to improve relationships with the Trump Administration.

The peer is said to have also floated the idea of Farage acting as a “bridge” between Elon Musk and “others.”

He would go on to suggest that Farage could serve the national interest in “weird and wonderful” ways.

A due diligence report, seen by the PM, and included in the files, quotes Mandelson as saying of Farage “you can’t ignore him.”

It went on: “He’s a bridgehead, both to President Trump and to Elon Musk and others . . . National interest is served in all sorts of weird and wonderful ways.”



‘Close relationship’ with Epstein

The bombshell diligence dossier also reveals the PM was explicitly warned Mandelson maintained “a particularly close relationship” with paedo financier Jeffrey Epstein.

It described Mandelson’s potential appointment to the role as a “reputational risk” because of his friendship with the paedophile financier.

The document also included links to news articles detailing Mandelson’s friendship with Epstein and noted that the ex-peer had stayed at Epstein’s home while he was in prison.

A 2019 report commissioned by JPMorgan found that Mandelson maintained a close relationship with Prince Andrew and Epstein.

The report mentioned Epstein’s own personal records – which showed contact beginning in 2002 and continuing throughout the 2000s.



The pair’s relationship began when Lord Mandelson was Business Minister and continued after the end of the Labour government, even after Epstein’s arrest, the report claims.

PM ignored warnings

While the Prime Minister has admitted that he “made a mistake” appointing Mandelson he has denied rampant claims of a “cover up.”

This is despite several senior figures warning Sir Keir Starmer about Mandelson’s friendship with Epstein.



Jonathan Powell and Sir Philip Barton, two of the UK’s most senior foreign policy officials were sceptical of Mandelson and raised concerns over his appointment.

Complaints were also raised with Morgan McSweeney about the appointment which felt “weirdly rushed”, Powell said.

Despite the concerns and the questions Sir Keir Starmer went ahead with the appointment and Mandelson was made Ambassador to the US.

Links to Russia & China

Inside the damning due diligence dossier there were also concerns raised around Mandelson’s “links to Russia and China.”

The report detailed Mandelson’s fondness of the idea of cosying up to Beijing – according to the document he had also pushed Chinese Communist Party talking points on Hong Kong.



Mandelson is also understood to have once served on the board of a company owned by a Russian defence firm.

RECOMMENDED STORIES

He held a non-executive director role on the board of Russian conglomerate Sistema, the majority shareholder of defence tech firm RTI.

RTI’s chairman was Yevgeny Primakov, a Putin ally and former Russian prime minister – Mandelson remained on the board until 2017, staying in the post after Russia‘s annexation of Crimea.

News.title: Heartbreaking health update for ‘The Girl in the Cellar’ Natascha Kampusch who was held as child sex slave for 8 years
News.content:
THE family of “The Girl in the Cellar” have revealed she is “completely gone” following her eight-year captivity as a child sex slave.

Natascha Kampusch, 38, was kidnapped aged 10 by sick Wolfgang Priklopil in 1998 before he kept her imprisoned in a basement for nearly a decade.





She was beaten hundreds of times a week, starved, and sexually abused before she made a daring escape in 2006.

Her heartbroken family have now shed light on her worsening condition – claiming she is “in a kind of prison again”.

As the 20th anniversary of her escape nears, her sister Claudia Nestelberger has said that her sibling is “completely gone”.

She told Austrian public broadcaster ORF: “Everyone knows how Natascha used to speak in front of the camera. That’s completely gone now.

“She’s mostly in her own world. She’s in a kind of prison again. It’s heartbreaking and we feel helpless.”

It comes after Natascha admitted she was “positive and hopeful” about her future just three years ago.

She was designing her own jewellery collection and involved in a hospital-building project in Sri Lanka.

She said at the time: “I spend a lot of time in nature and with my horse.”



Natascha’s story captured worldwide media attention, both for the duration of her captivity and her eventual escape.

After her release, she wrote several books, including a memoir detailing her experiences and struggles in adjusting to life after captivity.

She has also spoken publicly about her ordeal, raising awareness about issues related to abduction and trauma.

Natascha was held captive by Priklopil for eight years and four months, a duration that inspired the title of her memoir, 3,096 Days.

During her captivity, Natascha was imprisoned in a house on the outskirts of Vienna, kept in a hidden dungeon approximately three metres below ground.



She was forced to cook and clean for Priklopil while living in a small cellar beneath his garage.

The day her life changed forever began like any normal day for a schoolchild; she headed to Brioschiweg Primary School, just as she had countless times before.

However, Wolfgang Priklopil was hiding in wait in his white Mercedes van, parked along her route.



As Natascha walked past the van, Priklopil pounced, grabbing her and dragging her inside.

Although she struggled fiercely, her attacker was physically stronger and easily overpowered her.

A 12-year-old witness reported seeing her being pulled into a white minibus by two men, though Natascha later clarified that she only encountered Priklopil.

She recounted the harrowing moments of her abduction: “I asked him if he was going to kill me or bury me in the woods, but he told me to keep quiet.

“I was wrapped in a blanket and then carried down to the basement.



“Then I was placed in the darkness, a dark room, the dungeon.

“I was in shock. It was pitch black.

“I kept on thinking that someone would come and find me, that my mum and school would be missing me.

“I was convinced the police would come and save me, like in the movies and on TV.”



The entrance to her cellar was cleverly concealed behind a cupboard, featuring a concrete door reinforced with steel.

The room had no windows and was soundproofed to muffle her screams.

Priklopil regularly starved Natascha to weaken her physically and prevent escape.

He instilled fear in her by claiming the house’s doors and windows were rigged with explosives, and he often asserted that he had a gun that he would use to kill her and the neighbours if she attempted to flee.



If she cried, he resorted to violence, further deepening her trauma.

Natascha’s eventual escape involved both careful observation and a moment of opportunity.

By that point, Wolfgang Priklopil had begun to trust her more, allowing her occasional privileges, like limited time outside the cellar and helping him with tasks around the house.

On the day of her escape, Natascha had been cleaning Priklopil’s red BMW sports car in the garden.

Priklopil received a phone call, which distracted him.



Seizing the opportunity, Natascha saw that he had stepped away and wasn’t closely watching her.

She left the vacuum cleaner running as a distraction, and when Priklopil walked away to take the call, she made a break for it.

She ran 200 metres through neighbouring gardens and a street, jumping fences, and asking passers-by to call the police, but they paid her no attention.



After running for some distance, she came across a neighbour’s house and begged for help.

The neighbour quickly called the police, and within a short time, authorities arrived, and Natascha was finally free.

Recalling the moment she tried to escape, she said: “I crept to the gate which was usually closed or blocked by heavy objects, but not on this day.

“I could hardly breathe. I felt solidified, as if my arms and legs were paralysed, jumbled images shot through me.

“Then I saw a woman in a garden house and knocked on her window and whispered ‘Please help me!’



RECOMMENDED STORIES

“She asked what I was doing in her garden and then called the police.”

Priklopil, a technician in his 30s at the time who lived in his mum’s home, jumped in front of a train at a nearby station after learning she had escaped.

News.title: Four of six US aircrew dead in plane crash after ‘colliding with refuelling jet’ over Iraq as search goes on for missing
News.content:
FOUR of the missing six crew members on board a US refuelling plane which crashed overnight have been confirmed dead.

A frantic search for the troops travelling in the KC-135 Stratotanker was launched after the aircraft was downed over Iraq in a suspected mid-air collision.





US Central Command said in a statement: “At approximately 2 pm ET on March 12, a U.S. KC-135 refuelling aircraft went down in western Iraq.

“Four of six crew members on board the aircraft have been confirmed deceased as rescue efforts continue.

It comes as…



“The circumstances of the incident are under investigation. However, the loss of the aircraft was not due to hostile fire or friendly fire.

“The identities of the service members are being withheld until 24 hours after next of kin have been notified.”

The desperate search for the remaining two missing crew members continues.

Their nationalities are yet to be confirmed.



A KC-135 Stratotanker was lost during operations over western Iraq on Thursday evening after reportedly crashing into a second tanker aircraft as part of Operation Epic Fury – sparking fears of multiple casualties.

CENTCOM confirmed the crash, saying: “US Central Command is aware of the loss of a US KC-135 refuelling aircraft.

“Two aircraft were involved in the incident. One of the aircraft went down in western Iraq, and the second landed safely – this was not due to hostile fire or friendly fire.”

But an Iranian proxy group has come out since claiming responsibility for downing the aircraft.

Islamic Resistance in Iraq released a statement saying they shot down the Stratotanker as it entered Iraqi airspace.



Washington is yet to comment on the claims as they continue to say the enemy is not believed to have struck the planes.

At least seven US troops have died in the conflict so far with up to 150 feared to have been wounded.

The war in Iran has now spilled over into Iraq as the Middle East continues to contend with Tehran’s aggression.

A major blaze broke out at a French air base in Erbil following a drone attack from Iran yesterday.

President Emmanuel Macron said one soldier died in the attack with another six injured after a helicopter was reportedly struck.



Elsewhere, the UAE’s glitzy skyline was set ablaze after Iran targeted Dubai’s financial centre with kamikaze drones.

Thick plumes of black smoke were seen billowing over the Emirati city’s skyline as a terrifying inferno broke out in the industrial area.

Red alert sirens were also heard after midnight at Turkey‘s Incirlik airbase -a key Nato facility which houses US troops.

Iran has already fired two ballistic missiles in Turkish airspace with fears growing the alarms were raised after a third may have been detected.

RECOMMENDED STORIES

It comes as Donald Trump issued another blistering attack on Iran.



He warned: “Watch what happens to these deranged scumbags today.”

News.title: Mum, 31, killed her baby by bashing head repeatedly on floor in ‘fit of rage’ before claiming attack was ‘accident’
News.content:
MONSTER MUM Mum, 31, killed her baby by bashing head repeatedly on floor in ‘fit of rage’ before claiming attack was ‘accident’

A MUM who killed her two-month-old baby by smashing its head into the floor while in a “fit of rage” has been jailed for life.

Zara Arsalan, 31, repeatedly banged the head of defenceless Harleen Bains on the floor having “lost her temper” in their Black Country home on July 23, 2020.



Although the assault lasted just a few seconds, the baby was left with “catastrophic injuries”, including a fractured skull and brain damage.



Arsalan, also known as Sharandeep Talwandi, told cops the death of her daughter was a “tragic accident”.

The mum has now been found guilty of murder and was jailed for life this week, with a minimum term of 19 years.

During sentencing at Coventry Crown Court on Thursday, Mr Justice Cavanagh KC said Harleen “should have had a long and happy life ahead of her”.

He said: “The death of a child at this young age is a tragedy.

“I’m sure that you (Arsalan) had no intent to harm Harleen until the very moment you did do. You acted in a sudden fit of rage.

“The violence was ferocious. Tiny though she was, the pain and fear Harleen would have suffered will have been truly terrible.

“In this case, not only did you shake Harleen with extreme force but you also hit her head with great force against a hard surface a number of times.

“No one could have done this to a tiny and fragile baby without appreciating that there was a real prospect that it would lead to her death, not just injury.



“I’m sure, therefore, that you did not care if you killed her or not.”

Arsalan lived with her daughter and Jatinder Bains, the father of their child at a property in West Bromwich, near Birmingham, the court heard.

In the weeks before the baby’s death, the relationship between Arsalan and Mr Bains had become “stormy”, some two years after they first got together.

Harleen had suffered a rib facture caused by her mum’s “rough handling” during a loss of temper, between three and six days before her death.

Arsalan had been finding being a new mum a “struggle”, but was described as having a “good mother and baby bond”, the court was told.



The judge said: “Harleen would have screamed or cried loudly for some minutes after you did this to her so you would have realised that you had hurt her.

“You knew, by that stage, that you were at risk of harming Harleen but you did not seek help or support.”

Just minutes after his baby suffered injuries at the hands of Arsalan, Mr Bains called the emergency services on July 23.

Harleen died the following day due to a brain injury which caused “catastrophic and irreversible damage”.

The baby also suffered multiple blunt force blows to her head, a broken collarbone, a fracture to her left leg and injury to her spine.



Medical evidence indicated the baby girl had been held “very tightly and roughly”, which had caused “oscillation” of her head and neck.

The court was told this would have been “far outside the range of normal handling”.

Mr Justice Cavanagh KC added: “The person doing it would have had no doubt that it would have caused injury to their child.”

He said Harleen’s head had been banged “very hard” against the floor “three or four times”, with the level of force used being “exceptionally severe”.

The judge said he believed the “trigger” for the violence was that Arsalan was “tired and stressed” and “Harleen would not stop crying”.



He added: “It’s absolutely clear that you lost your temper with her.”

Mr Bains took his own life in August 2022, the court heard.

Arsalan, who had no previous convictions, gave “conflicting and untruthful” accounts to police about what had happened to her baby.

In 2020, she claimed that Harleen had “wriggled” out of her arms and onto the floor as she changed her.

Then in 2022, when she was interviewed having learned of Mr Bains’ death she lied again, claiming that Mr Bains had assaulted both her and Harleen.



Mr Justice Cavanagh KC said: “This was a false narrative, designed to throw blame upon Mr Bains.”

He added “There is no suggestion that Arsalan and Mr Bains were jointly responsible for the assault on Harleen.”

Detective Chief Inspector Phil Poole, from the West Midlands Police’s Homicide Unit, said: “This is an incredibly tragic case. Harleen was only two months old when she died at the hands of the person who should have been doing everything to keep her safe.

“Arsalan has never taken responsibility for her actions. Instead, she has repeatedly lied about what happened to Harleen despite being shown evidence that rejects her claims.

RECOMMENDED STORIES

“I want to thank all of the officers involved as I know they were deeply affected by this investigation. They have worked incredibly hard to get justice for Harleen and her family.



“I hope today’s sentence finally brings Harleen’s family some peace.”

News.title: ISIS-supporting Old Dominion attacker ‘targeted ROTC class’ killing decorated US veteran before hero cadets took him out
News.content:
A DECORATED US veteran who served several tours in the Middle East was killed when an ISIS-supporting gunman opened fire in his ROTC class at Old Dominion University.

Mohamed Bailor Jalloh, 36, was taken down by hero cadets on Thursday after storming the Reserve Officers’ Training Corps (ROTC) classroom and reportedly shouting “allahu akbar” or “God is great” before opening fire.





ROTC instructor Lt. Col. Brandon Shah has been identified as the man killed in the attack that also left two injured, both of whom are now stable, confirmed FBI Special Agent Dominique Evans.

The suspected terror attack was brought to an end when hero cadets took down the gunman, with one fatally stabbing Jollah, a naturalized US citizen originally from Sierra Leone, reported The New York Post.

They showed “extreme bravery and courage in containing the shooter and stopping further loss of life,” said Evans.

Decorated veteran killed

Lt. Col. Brandon Shah was a decorated chair of Old Dominion University’s military science department and served several tours in the Middle East with the US Army since 2003.

His deployments included supporting Operation Iraqi Freedom, Operation Enduring Freedom, and a rotation to Atlantic Resolve.

In his service to the country, Shah collected more than 17 awards including the Air Medal of Valor.

“You build such a good brother and sisterhood [in the military] and you can tell just by interacting with some individuals that they’re gonna be somebody, they’re gonna do some things that are great for our fellow man and the country and the world, and Brandon was exactly that,” former colleague Jason Fedish told WTKR News 3.



“When something tragic like this happens, to be able to hang your hat knowing that you died doing what you love and made a great impact is a fantastic thing.”

He also served with the Virginia National Guard, where Jalloh was also formerly a member before the shooter was honorably discharged in April 2015.

Two years later, Jalloh was sentenced to 11 years behind bars for offering material to support terror group ISIS. He was released from prison in December 2024.

Shooter said he wanted to carry out terror attack

The FBI is still determining whether the ROTC program was deliberately targeted by the ex-National Guard member.

It comes amid reports that Jalloh asked someone on campus if the class was for the ROTC and started shooting once someone confirmed it was, reported The New York Post.



Authorities are now attempting to retrace Jalloh’s steps leading up the attack to determine if anyone supported him.

Before his jail sentence, Jalloh met with members of the terror group during a six-month trip to Africa in 2016.

He also reportedly alluded to carrying out his own terror attack inspired by the 2009 mass shooting in Ft. Hood, Texas which killed 13 people and saw the killer shout “allahu akbar.”

According to the DOJ, during the 2016 trip, Jalloh told an FBI source he would carry out an attack during Ramadan which ends on March 19 this year.



Students heard yelling and screaming

Tributes have poured in for the slain ROTC instructor as terrified students recall the shocking moment gunfire erupted at the university.

“All of a sudden, I hear like just a bunch of bodies hitting each other like this loud altercation,” a student told 13NewsNow.

“People started running out of the building…there was yelling, screaming.”

Another student told WAVY she heard something “pop” followed by screams.

“I thought first it was like boys, you know, playing around, you know how they chase each other, but then I started hearing people say ‘Shooter. Shooter’,” she said.



Students and staff were on lock down for around half an hour before being given the all-clear.

“Lt. Col. Shah didn’t just lead a life of service to our country, he taught and led others to follow that path,” Virginia Governor Abigail Spangberger said in a statement.

RECOMMENDED STORIES

“I am grateful for his example, deeply saddened by his death, and praying for his family.”

An investigation into the attack continues.

News.title: ‘Barbaric’ couple jailed after forcing children into animal enclosures & making one eat cow tongue at Glasgow flat
News.content:
EVIL ABUSE ‘Barbaric’ couple jailed after forcing children into animal enclosures & making one eat cow tongue at Glasgow flat

AN ELDERLY couple who carried out horrific historical abuse towards six of their foster children have been jailed for a total of 60 months.

Douglas Daniel, 77, and Barbara Daniel, 76, tormented the kids -aged between four and 14 – at the property in Glasgow’s Parkhead.



The pair dished out horrific punishment methods which included forcing some of the youngsters into an animal pen.



Another victim was made to stand with geese who pecked her skin while a further girl was ordered to bathe in a bird bath.

Some of the kids went hungry under the Daniels roof but one recalled being fed eat cow tongue meant for pet ferrets by “evil” Barbara Daniel.

The Daniels, now of Kent, denied the accusations which spanned between 1986 and 1991.

But their brave victims’ testimonies decades later were vital as they were found guilty at Glasgow Sheriff Court to six charges of cruel and unnatural treatment of a child.

They Daniels were jailed for 30 months each at the sentencing earlier on Monday.

Sheriff Louise Arrol KC told them: “These children were removed from their primary caregivers and given to you as an emergency placement for their safety.

“What we learned what that these children were unsafe in their placement with you.

“The abuse you perpetrated on them was emotional, physical and psychological.



“Your treatment of these children was to install fear and terror which is nothing short of barbaric.

“The lifelong effect is unquantifiable. These children were in desperate need for nurture and care and love.

“Depriving them of this has resulted in a myriad of life changes. The impact on each of them is absolutely clear.

“I commend their bravery in coming forward to relive their life altering trauma.

“Their victim impact statements describe the abuse suffered has shaped every part of their lives negatively. The effects are still enduring.



“In short, you robbed them of their childhood.

“You were in a position of trust, apparently safeguarding vulnerable children.

“More than that you chose to act in the role of parent for these children.

“The behaviour you engaged in towards the children in your care was clearly degrading and humiliating.”



The court heard that the Daniels started fostering the first of their 100 children in 1986.

A 48-year-old woman told how she and her younger sister – then aged nine and four – went to live with them.

She recalled an incident when her sister – who was afraid of chickens – was held in a coop at the back of the property.

The woman said: “They locked the door from the outside.

“I tried to get her as my sister was screaming.”



When asked by prosecutor Ryan Watson who put her sister in the chicken coop, the woman replied: “Douglas.”

She added that while this went on, Barbara Daniel pulled her hair and helped Douglas Daniel close their door.

Mr Watson told jurors in his evidence: “The four-year-old was desperate to get out and was being pushed back inside.

“She was petrified, screaming to get out and she says to this day she cannot take her own children to a farm due to that experience.”

A 53-year-old woman stated in her evidence that Barbara Daniel made her stand with geese as a form of punishment.



She said: “When I spoke back to the Daniels or Barbara would get me to eat food that I disliked, geese eggs.

“If I refused, part of my punishment was I would be put outside where the geese were running free.

“The geese would attack you. She would pull you and shake you. Barbara would put you in front of the geese.

“The geese could tell you were scared of them and attack you.”

She stated that she was left with red marks “that would not go away” as the geese would nip her skin.



One of the woman told how she was also made to bathe outside.

Mr Watson said: “This was a bath where the birds would bathe or defecate in. Barbara would push her in and laugh at her

“She was only allowed to come out when Barbara had her fun.”

The same woman recalled being served a Sunday roast before it was taken away by Barbara Daniel.

Mr Watson added: “They then gave her cow’s tongue which due for the ferrets.



“She remembers I was the most disgusting thing she ever seen.

“She recalled that it was grey and saw the taste buds of the tongue.”

Some of the victims told jurors that they were not fed by the Daniels.

Another victim claimed she ate from the kitchen floor.

Mr Watson said: “She was looked on as the same as the dogs by the Daniels.”



A 47-year-old man who stayed with the Daniels told jurors that Barbara was seen as the “boss” while Douglas was a “scary physical man.”

The man recalled instances where he was forced to stand outside in the heat against a wall.

Douglas also attacked one of the girls by slapping them and ordered a victim to strip naked to check for injuries.

Barbara Daniel’s violence included hitting one of the girls with a wooden spoon and pulling a victim by her hair.

The victims told jurors that their personal freedoms were restricted by being locked in their rooms and the windows alarmed.



A woman recalled not being able to go to the toilet alone with the door locked.

One of the victims stated that she was only given one ply of toilet paper to use.

Mr Watson said: “They punished these children and showed no compassion or cared. They were the real animals.

“This was supposed to be a safe home, respite from the troubles in the other aspects of their life. The Daniels did not offer them that basic, simple and expected need.”

Douglas Daniel in his evidence denied the allegations put to him by his counsel Duncan McPhie.



The retired car mechanic said: “They never happened. There is no truth to any of them.”

RECOMMENDED STORIES

Duncan McPhie, defending Douglas Daniel, told the sentencing that his client continues to profess his innocence.

Alistair Sloan, defending Barbara Daniel, accepted his client’s position that she was the full time care giver.

News.title: Labour's cost of living tsar's very blunt warning to 'opportunistic' fuel firms
News.content:
Labour's cost of living tsar's very blunt warning to 'opportunistic' fuel firms

The government's new cost of living champion has called on regulators to get tough with any energy firms found "ripping off" consumers amid Middle East crisis

View 5 Images Pain at the pump - but how much of it is justified? (Image: ANP/Shutterstock)

Fuel firms are in the line of fire as the government’s cost of living tsar accused some of “taking the p!$$”.

‌



Chancellor Rachel Reeves and Energy Secretary Ed Miliband are due to meet industry bosses at Downing Street on Friday to hammer home the message that they will “not tolerate” profiteering from the rising price of oil.

‌



Richard Walker, the boss of frozen food chain Iceland and PM’s recently appointed cost of living champion, went further.

‌



Lord Walker, who will also attend the meeting, wrote: “Some companies are taking the p!$$. Today, we’re bringing the energy companies and fuel retailers into No10 to tell them that opportunistic rip-offs will not be tolerated.

"We will also hold the regulators to account and make sure they are doing their job by pulling ever lever available to them to protect consumers.”

View 5 Images Motorists filling up at a Shell petrol station in Dedworth, Windsor, on Wednesday (Image: Maureen McLean/Shutterstock)

‌



The nationwide average for unleaded has reached 140.15p per litre, up more than 7p since before the war began. Diesel has risen even faster, surging nearly 16p a litre to 158.23p.

It came as oil prices remained at around $100 a barrel - the first time it has settled there since August 2022 - as Iran stepped up attacks on infrastructure and shipping in the Gulf.

Mr Miliband did not rule out the government providing direct support or extending the freeze on fuel duty if the conflict continued.

‌



View 5 Images Russia may have raked in up to £1.4billion in oil taxes to help fund President Putin's war in Ukraine, it is claimed (Image: POOL/AFP via Getty Images)

He also criticised those calling on Labour to water down its net zero targets, saying: “We’ve got to have clean, homegrown power that we control. That’s the biggest long-term lesson of this crisis.”

The war has also seen mortgage rates jump amid fears of energy costs leading to higher inflation. Industry experts Moneyfacts revealed the average cost of a new five year fixed rate mortgage has now hit a near 12 month high of 5.19%.

‌



The average two-year fix has jumped to 5.10%. To make matters worse for borrowers, the number of fixed deals on offer has crashed by 530 since the chaos caused by the conflict began.

The Bank of England is expected to keep its base rate on hold next week, when before the crisis it had been poised to announce another cut.

View 5 Images Missile attacks on Gulf oil facilities have sent wholesale prices soaring (Image: Getty)

‌



Meanwhile, there are fears that the energy shock could result in the UK’s already fragile economy slowing further this year - or even going into recession. Data from the Office for National Statistics showed it failed to grow at all in January - even before any impact from the war.

Oil prices are on track to have risen 10% this week despite the International Energy Agency agreeing the record release of 400 million barrels from reserves. The IEA also warned the war was creating the largest supply disruption in the history of the global oil market.

It comes as Iran steps up attacks on merchant ships in the vital Strait of Hormuz. Tehran has warned the world to get ready for oil at $200 a barrel.

‌



View 5 Images Labour's newly appointed cost of living champion, Lord Walker, warns "opportunistic rip-offs it will not be tolerated" (Image: Adam Gerrard / Daily Mirror)

Meanwhile, Russia has reportedly raked in more than £1billion from soaring oil prices to help finance its war machine in Ukraine.

Moscow is estimated to be earning up to $150million (£113million) a day from the taxes collected on Russian oil exports.

Article continues below

Demand has been fuelled by an effective blockade of oil shipments through the Strait of Hormuz. According to the Financial Times, Russia may have collected between $1.3billion (almost £1billion) and $1.9billion (£1.4billion) since the Middle East war erupted.

The windfall comes as President Putin continues his costly war in Ukraine. Yet Gulf state oil producers have lost billions of dollars in energy revenues since the start of the conflict.

News.title: All UK dog owners given three week warning
News.content:
All UK dog owners given three week warning

Millions of dog owners across the UK are being warned

View Image Dog owners are being urged to look out for a number of signs (Image: Elena Grigorovich via Getty Images)

Dog owners across Britain are being urged to take extra care, as a three-week countdown begins.

‌



Easter weekend begins on Good Friday, April 3, or in three weeks' time. Easter falls on Sunday, April 5, and while the occasion typically brings festivities including egg hunts and various celebrations, those with dogs are being warned about certain particular Easter treats that pose serious dangers to canines. Pet insurance company Purely Pets is alerting dog owners to the hazards certain festive foods present to our four-legged companions, and advising on appropriate action should the worst happen.

‌



Chocolate

‌



A quintessential Easter treat, every variety of chocolate must be kept well beyond your dog's reach. Chocolate harbours a chemical called theobromine that's toxic to dogs, impacting the gut, heart, central nervous system and kidneys.

Even the foil wrapping around eggs presents a danger if swallowed, potentially becoming trapped in your dog's oesophagus or intestine.

Hot cross buns

‌



Another seasonal favourite, traditional hot cross buns feature dried fruit, including currants, sultanas, and raisins, all of which are poisonous to dogs. If consumed, they can trigger digestive issues and in severe instances, kidney failure.

Artificial sweeteners

Commonly found in home baking ingredients and certain Easter eggs, pet owners are urged to be wary of an artificial sweetener called xylitol. Dogs have a high sensitivity to xylitol, and even minuscule amounts can trigger severe complications.

‌



Dough and raw yeast

With many Brits set to spend the extended Easter weekend whipping up hot cross buns, bread, and pastries, it's important to remember that raw dough and yeast pose a significant risk to dogs. Yeast continues to ferment inside a dog's stomach, leading to painful bloating and potentially fatal gastric problems.

The fermentation process also generates alcohol, another toxin that can inflict serious harm on dogs.

‌



Meat bones

Whilst an Easter Sunday roast is a staple for many households, pet owners should be mindful of the hazards posed by bones from roasted meats like ham, beef, or lamb. These bones can splinter, resulting in choking, obstructions, or severe damage to a dog's digestive tract.

What are the symptoms of poisoning in dogs?

If you suspect your pooch has nabbed a nibble from the Easter spread, here are some common signs of poisoning to watch out for:

‌



Vomiting

Diarrhoea

Hyperactivity or restlessness

Tremors, seizures, or fits

Blood in the stool

What should you do if you think your dog has been poisoned?

"If you have seen your dog eat something they shouldn't have, it's advisable to call your vet immediately, rather than waiting to see if symptoms appear," advises Ross Hallifax from Purely Pets. "Poisoning can occur in as little as four hours after consumption, so it's always best to seek help from your vet as soon as possible.

"Try and make a note of the suspected substance your dog has ingested as this will help the vet identify the best course of treatment."

Article continues below

Which Easter foods can our dogs safely enjoy?

While numerous treats should be kept well away from your pooch, plenty of Easter favourites are perfectly fine for them to tuck into. These include roast lamb, provided it's boneless and hasn't been seasoned with garlic or onions, plain cooked spuds, and certain vegetables, such as carrots (avoid cutting into rounds), green beans, peas, broccoli, sweetcorn, and cauliflower.

News.title: Secrets of Meghan's break-up with Netflix exposed by insiders - with brutal Harry warning
News.content:
EXCLUSIVE: Secrets of Meghan's break-up with Netflix exposed by insiders - with brutal Harry warning

Meghan Markle's As Ever lifestyle brand is no longer working with Netflix it was confirmed last week - but there are conflicting reports as to the reasons why they parted company

View 6 Images Meghan launched As Ever in 2025, supported by Netflix (Image: Emma Grede/Youtube)

Industry insiders have unpicked the mystery surrounding Meghan Markle's collapsed deal with Netflix.

‌



On Friday, it was announced that the Duchess' lifestyle brand As Ever would become fully independent, having previously been supported financially by the streaming giant.

‌



But there is confusion over their parting of ways, with conflicting reports emerging from both camps. Shortly after the announcement, a source seemed to suggest that ending the partnership was Meghan's idea rather than Netflix's.

‌



They said: "Meghan is still on good terms with the Netflix team and close personal friends with Ted (Sarandos) so hasn't wanted to upset him, but is very happy to have full control of the company. It's a good time for Meghan to have complete control, given recent successes."

View 6 Images Meghan's lifestyle brand sells wine and jam

The source further suggested to The Sun that Meghan felt 'held back' by the agreement. "Netflix have been a good partner, but she's wanted to go global with the brand for some time, but has been held back by the more cautious Netflix team."

‌



But this week, an alternative version came to light. A source based in Los Angeles told the Mail that Netflix were "not happy" with how Meghan's brand was performing. "They were not happy with the fact that no one really cared about the brand - so when they were looking to create As Ever areas in Netflix House there was no appetite for it.

Speaking of other programmes released on the streaming service, they added: "It just didn't fit with Squid Game or Stranger Things or Bridgerton like they had hoped".

So what's really going on behind the scenes in Meghan's world? Mayah Riaz, communications guru and PR to the stars, tells The Mirror: "This is really interesting, because when you see two very different narratives emerging around the same split, it is usually a sign that both sides are trying to manage the optics.

‌



"In high profile partnerships such as this, perception is almost as important as the deal itself. Netflix will naturally want to position this decision as being strategic, while Meghan's team will of course want to frame it as her taking control of her brand rather than her being dropped."

View 6 Images The brand was also linked to her series with Netflix, With Love, Meghan (Image: Netflix)

Explaining the potential reasons behind the split, the expert adds: "I have seen this so many times and it'd not be unusual if there is an element of damage control happening on both sides. The reality is that streaming platforms are becoming far more ruthless about who and what they invest in. Being a celebrity alone is no longer enough. Content has to deliver audiences, buzz and commercial return. If a project does not clearly do that, it’s sad but true that even very big names can find themselves vulnerable.

‌



"From a PR perspective, the Sussex brand has always relied heavily on momentum and narrative. Looking at the early years, there was intense global curiosity around them. Now we are seeing a shift where the public and platforms alike are asking a much simpler question: what is the actual product?

"I think it's also important to mention that there is a wider fatigue around celebrity projects that feel more like personal branding exercises rather than compelling entertainment. If Netflix felt the concept behind As Ever did not translate into strong viewing or global appeal, it would make sense that they would stepped back from it.

View 6 Images A PR expert has warned there could be trouble looming for the Sussexes (Image: PA)

‌



"At the same time, Meghan positioning the split as her protecting her brand is smart reputationally. This keeps the narrative focused on independence and control rather than being rejected."

In their statement last week, Netflix suggested that they always intended to part ways from As Ever, saying: "Meghan's passion for elevating everyday moments in beautiful yet simple ways inspired the creation of the As Ever brand, and we are glad to have played a role in bringing that vision to life.

"As it was always intended, Meghan will continue growing the brand and take it into its next chapter independently, and we look forward to celebrating how she continues to bring joy to households around the world."

‌



Meanwhile, an As Ever spokesperson said: "As Ever is grateful for Netflix's partnership through launch and our first year. We have experienced meaningful and rapid growth and As Ever is now ready to stand on its own. We have an exciting year ahead and can't wait to share more."

View 6 Images Harry has made rare appearances in Meghan's projects (Image: Netflix)

But Mayah believes there's more ay play - and it could spell bad news for both Meghan and Harry. "I personally think the bigger issue for the Sussexes is that the intrigue factor that once surrounded them is not as strong as it once was," she warns. "And this matters because attention is currency, and attention spans move quickly. For their brand to stay powerful, the projects need to feel bigger than the headlines.

‌



"One thing is certain though: in the world of celebrity PR, when both sides are rushing to explain a break-up, it means the story behind the scenes is more complicated than either version suggests."

The move follows last summer's announcement of a much watered-down relationship between the Sussexes and Netflix over producing TV series for the platform. In August, their contract was downgraded to a less lucrative "first look deal" that gives Netflix first refusal on any new projects - but allows Harry and Meghan to then take their work elsewhere.

Ha rry and Meghan signed a multiyear production deal with Netflix in September 2020 in a bid to achieve financial independence in their post-royal lives. Their six-part docuseries Harry & Meghan became a major hit for the platform - but follow-up projects failed to resonate, including Live to Lead, Heart of Invictus, a docuseries centered on Prince Harry's Invictus Games, and Polo.

‌



View 6 Images Harry and Meghan's docuseries on Netflix proved to be a hit - but they are yet to match its success (Image: Netflix)

Then in March 2025 came the first season of With Love, Meghan which showed her cooking, entertaining and crafting with celebrity friends, including Chrissy Teigen.

It was widely panned and the second season and a holiday special failed to make the top 1,000 shows on Netflix in 2025. It has since been confirmed it will not be returning for a third series.

Article continues below

As Ever launched in 2025, supported by Netflix. The streamer was "not just as my partner in the show, but as my partner in my business, which was huge," Meghan said last year. The brand was linked to With Love, Meghan, with hopes As Ever stock would prove a hit with fans.

Now that she's gone solo, Meghan has big plans to take the brand global. It remains to be seen whether she can succeed.

News.title: Locals puzzled as strange structures pop up on popular UK beach
News.content:
Locals puzzled as strange structures pop up on popular UK beach

Huge steel supports have appeared on Talacre beach in Flintshire - and locals have been left bemused by the temporary structures

View 3 Images Construction of a cofferdam on the beach, through which a tunnel will be drilled under the dunes to accommodate the combined electric and fibre-optic conduit (Image: Linda Millwall)

A vast collection of steel supports has sprung up along the iconic Talacre beach on the Flintshire coast. The sight has left locals and visitors alike scratching their heads, with some branding the structures a "right eyesore".

‌



The structures are part of laying a new pipeline to the nearby Point of Ayr gas terminal, a component of the multi-billion-pound HyNet project. Captured carbon emissions (CO2) from industrial facilities in North Wales and northwest England will be transported to the revamped terminal, then piped beneath the sea to exhausted offshore gas reservoirs in Liverpool Bay. However, these are merely temporary installations set to be dismantled come April.

‌



The existing natural gas pipeline at Point of Ayr, extending from Flint to the offshore Douglas platform, is being repurposed to carry CO2 out to sea. Nevertheless, a fresh "conduit" is required between the terminal and platform – this will accommodate a combined electric and fibre-optic cable.

‌



"This cable will supply the power and upgraded telecommunications needed to support the receipt and distribution of CO2 to the storage sites," a HyNet spokesperson explained, reports North Wales Live.

The steel supports will facilitate the assembly of sections of the underground conduit. Utilising "trenchless" horizontal directional drilling (HDD), the finished conduit will subsequently be threaded back under Talacre's towering dunes towards the Point of Ayr terminal.

View 3 Images The steel stands disappear into the distance (Image: Vicki Smith)

‌



HyNet explained: "Trenchless techniques are used to lay cables under rivers, roads, and areas of conservation like sand dunes, without digging a trench through them.

"HDD allows us to lay cables underground without disturbing the land above and means that we only need to dig small exit and entry pits on either side of the area."

One of these excavation points is already visible on the shoreline. A cofferdam is being constructed using temporary steel sheet piles to contain any drilling fluids.

‌



It will be dismantled once operations conclude at the end of August 2026.

View 3 Images The steel supports are expected to be removed in early April (Image: Vicki Smith)

This summer, cabling will be transported from the platform to the shore by vessel. This will be drawn through the conduit by a cable-laying ship moored offshore.

‌



A public footpath surrounding Point of Ayr has been shut for the conduit operations. A spokesperson stated: "The conduit for the cable installation will take place between February and April 2026.

"Following this, a cable-laying vessel will be used to connect the cable from the beach area to the New Douglas Offshore CCS Platform. This cable pull is expected to take place from June 2026 to August 2026."

Temporary barriers have been erected to shield walkers from dust, and to prevent onlookers peering in. HyNet confirmed all apparatus, including fencing, screens and signage, will be cleared once the works are complete.

‌



On the shoreline, the conduit will be buried beneath sand, and any remaining kit will be recovered. "This means that there will be no visible infrastructure on the beach when construction is complete," HyNet confirmed.

Another key aspect of the Carbon Capture and Storage (CCS) programme currently under scrutiny is the "Main Onshore Pipeline" stretching from Stanlow, near Ellesmere Port, to Flint. This project involves the installation of a 36-inch diameter pipe running through Deeside, Ewloe Green, Northop Hall and Flint Mountain.

Plans also include three spur pipelines, one of which will connect Northop Hall to the Hanson Padeswood cement plant near Mold. Topsoil stripping – a necessary step for future restoration – is set to commence this month along the Main Onshore Pipeline route.

Article continues below

Traffic management measures are already in effect at Northop Hall. Additional drop-in community information events are being organised for the project team to provide updates (see dates below).

Drop-in community information events (all 4pm-7pm).

News.title: Turkey chaos as NATO intercept ballistic missile heading for holiday hotspot
News.content:
NATO defences have intercepted a ballistic missile heading for Turkey.

Turkey said the missile launched from Iran had entered Turkish airspace before it was destroyed by NATO air defences.

Ensure our latest headlines always appear at the top of your Google Search by making us a Preferred Source. Click here to activate or add us as your Preferred Source in your Google search settings.

Turkey's defence ministry said a missile launched from Iran had been destroyed by Nato "assets", in a post this morning.

"A ballistic munition launched from Iran and entering Turkish airspace was neutralised by Nato air and missile defence assets deployed in the eastern Mediterranean," the ministry says in a statement posted on X.

It says that "all necessary measures are being taken decisively" towards threats against the country and its airspace.

This is the third time NATO air defences have shot down Iranian missiles targeted at Turkey. Last Wednesday (March 4) the Turkish defence ministry said a missile launched from Iran was detected passing through Iraq and Syria before heading towards Turkish airspace, where it was quickly destroyed.

Meanwhile on Monday (March 9) NATO defences based in Turkey destroyed another missile, with fragments landing in empty fields in Gaziantep, in southeast Turkey.

"We once again emphasise that all necessary steps will be taken resolutely and without hesitation against any threat directed at our country's territory and airspace," the Turkish defence ministry said after that incident.

The news comes as countries across the Middle East continue to be pounded by Iranian strikes across the region following the US-Israeli attack on Iran last week which saw supreme leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei killed.

Two people have been killed in Oman after the downing of a drone in Sohar province, Oman’s state news agency is reporting this morning.

Meanwhile Israel’s Health Ministry says at least 2,975 people have been injured in the attacks since the US-Israel war on Iran started on February 28.

Saudi Arabia's defence ministry says it destroyed at least 28 drones entering its airspace in a series of posts on X overnight.

Debris from an Iranian kamikaze drone has also struck Dubai’s financial district this morning as the war continues to wreak havoc across the region.

News.title: Newborn baby dies with fractured skull after mum's 'fit of rage'
News.content:
A mum who killed her two-month-old baby has been jailed for life.

Zara Arsalan, 31, from West Bromwich, killed her daughter Harleen Bains by repeatedly banging the baby's head on the floor while "in a fit of rage" at their Black Country home on July 23, 2020.

Emergency services were called after a report that a baby girl was unresponsive following a fall from her mother's lap. When paramedics arrived at the home in West Bromwich, Harleen’s breathing was unstable and she had suffered a serious head injury.

The baby died in hospital the following day from her injuries, which police said were inconsistent with a fall and were more likely caused by "coming into contact with a hard surface".

A post-mortem examination found Harleen had sustained several skull fractures, bleeding on the brain, broken ribs and a broken collarbone. Police also said she had likely been shaken.

Arsalan was convicted after a trial last month and was sentenced to life imprisonment with a minimum term of 19 years at Coventry Crown Court on Thursday.

Sentencing Arsalan, Mr Justice Cavanagh KC told her: "You acted in a sudden fit of rage.

"However, I consider that the scale and nature, the sheer ferocity, of the violence that was inflicted upon Harleen is a significant aggravating factor."

He added: "The violence was ferocious and, tiny though she was, the pain and fear that Harleen will have suffered for the short time between the commencement of the assault and when she was rendered unconscious will have been truly terrible."

Detective Chief Inspector Phil Poole, from the West Midlands Police's Homicide Unit, described the case as "incredibly tragic".

He said: "Harleen was only two months old when she died at the hands of the person who should have been doing everything to keep her safe.

"Arsalan has never taken responsibility for her actions. Instead, she has repeatedly lied about what happened to Harleen despite being shown evidence that rejects her claims.

"I want to thank all of the officers involved as I know they were deeply affected by this investigation. They have worked incredibly hard to get justice for Harleen and her family. I hope today’s sentence finally brings Harleen’s family some peace."

News.title: 'I woke up with tingly toes and ended up paralysed'
News.content:
'I woke up with tingly toes and ended up paralysed'

The stay-at-home mum was told she has a condition that affects the nerves, senses, movement and things such as breathing and heartbeat

View 4 Images Ali and her daughter, Meredith

A woman who woke up with “tingly” toes says her body slowly became numb and she ended up paralysed. Ali Sudderth says her toes were tingling and slightly numb before the numbness progressively spread across her body and left her unable to move for three months.

‌



The 33-year-old says she spoke to a doctor and went to the hospital in the days leading up to her paralysis as she started to feel numb and tingly - but was given no answers. “I went to carry my daughter down the stairs and felt so off balance I told my husband we needed to go to the hospital as soon as possible,” said Ali, from Athens, Georgia, US. “I went and was diagnosed that night.”

‌



The stay-at-home mum was told she has Guillain-Barré syndrome, a rare condition that affects the nerves, senses, movement and things such as breathing and heartbeat. Some people’s symptoms become so severe that they are not able to move their legs, arms and face.

‌



View 4 Images Ali was in hospital for three months

Ali, who is mum to three-year-old daughter Meredith, and runs a cookie decorating business, visited hospital on a Saturday - and was paralysed by Monday. She said: “They actually caught it really early and the fact that I came in before I was paralysed was a blessing. They started my treatment that night (which aids in recovery but can’t stop the disease) and I was paralysed by Monday.”

That weekend was the first time Ali had spent more than two hours away from her young daughter - but would not be the last. Ali spent three months in hospital recovering and learning how to walk again.

‌



She was so weak that she struggled to even raise her phone, or lift her daughter up. She said: “I cried myself to sleep so many nights. It is heartbreaking to watch everyone around you be able to take care of your child while you helplessly watch. I couldn’t even change her diaper. All I wanted to do was hold her on my own.”

View 4 Images Ali in physiotherapy

Ali was allowed to leave hospital in October 2024 and has recently run a marathon - crossing the finish line with her daughter in her arms. While Guillain-Barré syndrome doesn’t affect Ali physically much any more, she does still get some slight tingling or numbness in her toes.

Article continues below

She added: “Mentally it has changed who I am as a person so in that way everything I do has been affected by Guillain-Barré I want to run more marathons; I honestly would love to run an ultramarathon.

View 4 Images Ali, her husband and daughter, Meredith

“I want to keep pushing my body to its limit and not waste the second chance I feel like I’ve been given. I want to keep pushing, getting stronger and showing my daughter what beautiful gifts our bodies are. I want to inspire other people and show them that there is hope no matter how dark their situation may seem.”

News.title: Diesel has jumped 16p in past fortnight but petrol only 7p - this is why
News.content:
Diesel has jumped 16p in past fortnight but petrol only 7p - this is why

Middle East conflict has driven up oil prices, leading to sharp increases in petrol and diesel costs. But diesel is rising faster than petrol - here's the reason.

View 3 Images Diesel has shot up much quicker than petrol (Image: Martin Zarkov via Getty Images)

Two weeks can be an eternity in geopolitics and the past fortnight's events have sent shockwaves through the global economy. US and Israeli strikes on Iran kicked off on February 28, causing oil prices to skyrocket overnight just as UK motorists were beginning to enjoy stable and reasonably priced petrol and diesel.

‌



This resulted in immediate price hikes at the pumps. While all drivers are feeling the pinch, those behind the wheel of diesel vehicles may be puzzled as to why their fuel costs have surged more rapidly than petrol.

‌



On Wednesday, the RAC reported that diesel prices had jumped nearly 9% since February 28. Petrol prices, on the other hand, were up by an average of 6%.

‌



This equates to an additional 7p per litre for petrol, while diesel has seen an average increase of 16p per litre during the same period.

RAC head of policy Simon Williams said on Thursday: "Drivers tell us the cost of motoring is a major concern and fuel is a huge contributor to that, so making sure they're paying a fair price at the pumps is essential. For that reason, we welcome the competition watchdog's scrutiny of what's happening on forecourts across the country.

"RAC fuel watch data shows average prices have rocketed in under two weeks, with the average price of petrol increasing by 7p to 140p a litre and diesel by 16p to 158p. This has added £4 and £8 to the cost of filling up a family car."

‌



View 3 Images Chancellor Rachel Reeves has been urged to take action (Image: Leon Neal, Getty Images)

With oil prices hovering around $100 per barrel on Friday morning, any relief for motorists in the UK and globally appears to depend on calming tensions in the latest conflict. There's also growing pressure on Chancellor Rachel Reeves to postpone her plans to gradually remove a 5p reduction to the duty, beginning with a 1p rise from September this year.

Edmund King, president of the AA, said: "As the conflict in the Middle East continues, the global increase in oil prices will hurt inflation, particularly with the diesel price hikes. As most goods and services are delivered by diesel vehicles, this will lead to price rises which the consumer will be stung with. We strongly encourage the Chancellor to delay the staggered reintroduction of the 5p fuel duty discount in order to offer some breathing space for hard-pressed households."

‌



Prime Minister Sir Keir Starmer has subsequently said the Government will keep the situation "under review" given the Middle East conflict.

Why diesel costs are climbing faster than petrol

Steven Greenall, protection adviser at Rayleigh-based Protect and Lend, outlined why diesel is increasing more rapidly than petrol.

He explained: "Put simply, diesel is a more complex carbon product. It is more expensive to refine from crude oil than petrol, the UK is more dependent on the importation of diesel than petrol, and margins are increased more than petrol at the pump."

‌



View 3 Images PM Sir Keir Starmer said the situation was 'under review' (Image: WPA Pool, Getty Images)

Rohit Parmar-Mistry, founder of Burton-on-Trent-based Pattrn Data, explained: "Diesel is not just 'oil plus tax'. At the pump it is driven by the middle distillate market (diesel, jet fuel, heating oil), and that market can tighten even when petrol is relatively well supplied. Right now diesel is likely seeing a wider 'crack' spread: refiners are charging more margin to turn crude into diesel because inventories are lower and supply is less flexible.

"HGVs, vans, construction and logistics keep demand steady, while jet fuel competes for the same refinery output. Maintenance or unplanned outages hit diesel harder. Geopolitics matters too. The current Iran war is pushing up risk premia via shipping disruption, insurance costs and shifting trade flows."

‌



Watchdog warns retailers over pricing

On Thursday, the competition watchdog announced it had placed fuel retailers "on notice" that it was intensifying scrutiny of petrol and diesel prices amid the Middle East conflict.

The Competition and Markets Authority (CMA) informed companies operating thousands of forecourts nationwide that it was accelerating formal requirements for them to provide revenue, costs and sales data. The regulator stated the action would accelerate its examination of fuel profit margins made by retailers since the conflict started.

The CMA confirmed it would also assess how rapidly petrol prices rise and fall in response to wholesale cost fluctuations, and whether there's evidence of so-called "rocket and feather" pricing - where costs surge swiftly when oil prices increase, but decline gradually when oil prices drop.

Article continues below

Whilst acknowledging that firms throughout the economy were likely facing considerable pressure from soaring energy costs, which could impact pricing, it emphasised that filling stations "should not exploit the situation", noting that any evidence of such behaviour would be highlighted in its pricing update, "which will be published as soon as possible".

The CMA's executive director for markets, Juliette Enser, said: "While price increases might be inevitable because of rising wholesale costs, it is important that those increases reflect genuine cost pressures. We will be closely scrutinising and reporting on what's happening with fuel prices and call out any concerning behaviour."

News.title: Jack Draper beaten after controversial umpire call
News.content:
Jack Draper was penalised with a controversial hindrance call as Daniil Medvedev ended the Briton's Indian Wells title defence in the quarter-finals.

Draper fell to a 6-1 7-5 defeat by former world number one Medvedev, but the Russian was booed after the tense moment midway through the second set.

World number 14 Draper, serving at 5-5 0-15, briefly stretched his arms out wide during a rally to signal he thought Medvedev's forehand had gone long.

The rally continued for another seven shots before Medvedev netted a backhand.

Two-time finalist Medvedev then asked umpire Aurelie Tourte for a video review to see whether Draper had been guilty of hindrance - making either an action or a noise to disturb an opponent.

After watching the replay several times on her tablet, Tourte told Draper she was ruling against him as "you did something different in the rally than you would normally do" and awarded Medvedev the point.

News.content: But she said she chose not to text them to see if they were safe, as a pinging sound might have given their locations away. Initially, the ramming was reported as an "active shooter incident".

News.title: How long will the cold weather last?
News.content:
Wintry weather is making a comeback across the UK as Arctic air limits temperatures to single digits with blustery winds and snow for some.

Parts of Scotland, including the central belt, saw a covering of snow through the Friday morning rush hour.

Despite weather warnings having now elapsed, snow showers are still likely over the hills in Scotland and Northern Ireland. Wintry scenes are also possible across the Lake District and the Pennines in England.

Through Friday there will be a mix of sunny spells with blustery showers - with hail and thunder also possible. The turbulent atmosphere means there could well be some fabulous clouds, along with odd rainbow.

News.content: And after viewing the two episodes, he said, "my flabber has never been so gasted", although he quipped: "I've never forgiven the BBC for losing those episodes, it would be really nice to get a few royalties."

Boxing.title: Jake Paul suffers embarrassing hygiene gaffe during Donald Trump rally appearance as major wardrobe error exposes long-held insecurity
Boxing.content:
YouTuber-turned-boxer Jake Paul was seen profusely sweating in an appearance with US President Donald Trump at a political rally.

Paul joined Trump on stage at Verst Logistics in Kentucky and was given the chance to address the crowd.

The 29-year-old went viral on social media, not for his comments about Trump and American manufacturing, but instead about his appearance.

Many pointed out vast sweat patches that were visible on Paul's beige suit as he spoke to the crowd.

The patches were particularly prominent under his armpits, which were exposed further when he raised his arms to the crowd.

Paul had previously spoken about how sweating had long been a major insecurity of his.

Jake Paul gets on stage at the Trump rally and has the nastiest pit stains I've ever seen



(it also appears the crowd doesn't know who he is) pic.twitter.com/3Yc071tZzf — Aaron Rupar (@atrupar) March 11, 2026

YouTuber-turned-boxer Jake Paul was seen profusely sweating in an appearance with US President Donald Trump

Paul and his brother Logan had promoted his deodorant brand ahead of his fight with Mike Tyson back in 2024

Paul made the revelation back in 2024 when he launched a deodorant brand W, which he and his brother Logan promoted before and after his bout with Mike Tyson.

'Sweating has been a longtime insecurity of mine,' Paul had said when launching the brand.

'If you know me, you know I'm a clean freak. I always want to smell fresh, but training in Puerto Rico's humid climate makes it a real challenge.

'I needed a product that could keep up with my lifestyle — one that smells amazing but isn't full of junk ingredients, and honestly, one that wasn't from 2006.

'That's why I started W… to create products for guys that will boost their confidence and smell amazing, but also don't cost $20 for a stick of deodorant.'

The W deodorants are claimed to offer 24 hours protection, with the brand website stating: 'With doubled scent load, increased hydration and more sweat-blocking power, our new deodorant and body wash are a monumental breakthrough for mankind (or at least for your morning)'.

During the event, Trump signalled that Paul's influence could eventually shift from the boxing ring to the halls of government in the coming years.

'I'm going to make a prediction that you will be, in the not too distant future, running for political office,' Trump told the fighter and the assembled supporters.

Join the debate Did YOU spot Jake Paul's hygiene gaffe?

Trump had brought Paul onstage during a rally on Wednesday in Kentucky

Trump suggested that Paul could shift from the boxing ring to the halls of government

'And you have my complete and total endorsement.'

Paul had expressed his admiration for Trump credited him with teaching him the fundamental values of grit and resilience.

'We need more factories like this thriving all over the country,' Paul added. And I know [Trump's] going to be the one to bring that here to us.

'I know God is with us. I know he wants us on the right side of history, and everyone here has to do their part.

'And God's got us. Trump's got us. God bless.'

Trump returned the praise, telling the crowd: 'He's a great guy. He's a courageous guy and a talented guy. He's a hell of a fighter, too, by the way.'

The endorsement of Paul's fighting spirit comes as the 29-year-old continues to dominate headlines following his endeavours in the boxing ring over recent years.

Boxing.title: Donald Trump gives Jake Paul shock endorsement for political career as boxer joins President at Kentucky rally
Boxing.content:
President Donald Trump brought boxing star Jake Paul onstage during a rally on Wednesday and made a bold prediction about the social media star's future.

The 29-year-old Ohio native, who has transitioned from a YouTube personality to a major force in the combat sports world, stood alongside the president as the crowd in Hebron, Ky roared in support.

During the event, Trump signaled that Paul's influence could eventually shift from the boxing ring to the halls of government in the coming years.

'I'm going to make a prediction that you will be, in the not too distant future, running for political office,' Trump told the fighter and the assembled supporters.

The president didn't stop at a mere prediction, immediately offering his political backing to the 'Problem Child' should he decide to pursue a seat in the future.

'And you have my complete and total endorsement,' the president added, cementing a public alliance with one of the most polarizing and popular figures in modern media.

President Donald Trump brought boxing star Jake Paul onstage during a rally on Wednesday

Trump suggested that Paul could shift from the boxing ring to the halls of government

Paul expressed his admiration for the president and credited him with teaching him the fundamental values of grit and resilience

Addressing the Kentucky crowd, Paul expressed his admiration for the president and credited him with teaching him the fundamental values of grit and resilience.

He told the crowd that the president had taught him the value of 'courage' and to 'never back down from a fight,' a mantra Paul has lived by throughout his boxing career.

'We need more factories like this thriving all over the country,' Paul said, emphasizing his support for the president's economic vision for the American Heartland.

'And I know [Trump's] going to be the one to bring that here to us,' he continued, signaling his belief in a resurgence of American manufacturing.

The fighter also leaned into a spiritual message, telling the audience: 'I know God is with us. I know he wants us on the right side of history, and everyone here has to do their part.'

'And God's got us. Trump's got us. God bless,' Paul concluded, before reflecting on the local spirit of the attendees in the Bluegrass State.

'I feel all the local Kentuckians feel the same way -- you guys have that fight, you guys have that swag. There's a lot of young kids in here. The future of America.'

Trump returned the praise, telling the crowd: 'He's a great guy. He's a courageous guy and a talented guy. He's a hell of a fighter, too, by the way.'

'We need more factories like this thriving all over the country,' Paul said, emphasizing his support for the president's economic vision

Paul's most recent endeavor in the boxing ring saw him take on British star Anthony Joshua

The endorsement of Paul's fighting spirit comes as the 29-year-old continues to dominate headlines following his endeavors in the boxing ring over recent years.

Paul has transformed the boxing landscape by leveraging his massive digital following to create events that have outdrawn traditional championship bouts in viewership and revenue.

His most recent endeavor in the ring saw him take on British heavyweight star Anthony Joshua, which ended in a knockout victory for 'AJ'.

Whether Paul follows Trump's advice and swaps the gloves for a suit remains to be seen.

Boxing.title: Anthony Joshua quits the UK to move to Dubai - even as thousands try to flee amid rising panic in Middle East warzone
Boxing.content:
Anthony Joshua quits the UK to move to Dubai - even as thousands try to flee amid rising panic in Middle East warzone

Boxing superstar Anthony Joshua is quitting the UK to move to Dubai - even though the desert city is now in a warzone with thousands of Britons desperate to get out.

The former heavyweight world champion, 36, has formally confirmed he will now be resident in the United Arab Emirates despite it currently coming under nightly attack from Iranian suicide drones.

Joshua, who was born in Watford of Nigerian heritage, won a gold medal at the 2012 London Olympics and has since been awarded both OBE and MBE honours - but now is quitting his home country.

The surprise move by 'AJ' - as he is known to fans - is revealed in the small print of paperwork he filed on Friday for his £150million business empire in which he is legally required to state the country in which he lives.

And, extraordinarily, the documentation shows that Joshua has pressed ahead with his decision to change his residential status despite the war between the USA and Israel and Iran which has seen neighbouring UAE come under sustained attack.

Anthony Joshua is moving to Dubai - as revealed in the small print of his paperwork

Footage taken from social media shows Saturday's drone strike on Dubai International Airport

As well as the weather and facilities, Dubai is popular with the wealthy because there is no income tax there.

Joshua would have been liable to pay UK tax on his dividend payment of £10.1million in 2024 and £6.3million in 2023.

His company Sparta Promotions also made profits of £20.396million and paid £6.65million in UK tax in 2024.

Joshua confirmed the decision in the same week that recent UK sport emigre Rio Ferdinand and his wife Kate spoke about what it is like to live in Dubai while it is under missile attack.

Those attacks have seen thousands of Britons living in or visiting Dubai when the war broke out left desperate to get out - with some paying as much as £100,000 to secure evacuation on a private jet.

Filings for two of Joshua's companies, Sparta Promotions Limited and 258 Investments Limited, both show the formal change in where he will from now on reside.

Joshua has long been a regular visitor to Dubai where he uses the renowned sporting facilities for holidays and training, as well as promotional work for brands he represents.

The most spectacular stunt in this vein came in 2017 when he undertook a training sparring session on the helipad of the famous sail-shaped Burj Al Arab hotel - a 90-foot wide landing platform suspended 700 feet above the sea.

Passengers in the terminal at Dubai Airport on Saturday after the airport closed again due to drone strikes
